claims.claim_text: The module framing says six words tripled AI reasoning ability.
claims.span_hint: frontmatter summary
claims.anchor_text: **Heureka Moment**: Six words that tripled AI's reasoning ability
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Heureka Moment**: A simple reasoning trigger can sharply improve benchmark performance
claims.rationale: The word count and the performance claim are presented too precisely for the cited literature actually discussed in the module.

claims.claim_text: The module presents a precise Tokyo/January 2022/3:15 AM origin story for chain-of-thought prompting.
claims.span_hint: section: The Six Words That Changed AI
claims.anchor_text: **Tokyo. January 2022. 3:15 AM.**
claims.rationale: This is a highly specific anecdotal dateline with no attributable primary source in the module.

claims.claim_text: Jason Wei personally tested PaLM with one tiny prompt change in the described scene.
claims.span_hint: section: The Six Words That Changed AI
claims.anchor_text: Jason Wei stared at his screen, exhausted but excited. He had just run the same math problem through Google's PaLM model with one tiny change: instead of asking for the answer directly, he added six words to the prompt.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Researchers were testing whether adding explicit reasoning cues to prompts could improve performance on math benchmarks.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed narrative scene about a real person and event rather than a citable statement from a primary paper.

claims.claim_text: A model jumped from 17% to 79% accuracy on grade-school math from that six-word change.
claims.span_hint: section: The Six Words That Changed AI
claims.anchor_text: The model that had been getting 17% accuracy on grade-school math problems suddenly jumped to 79%. Not from better training data. Not from a bigger model. Just six words.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Early chain-of-thought papers reported large gains on reasoning benchmarks when models were prompted to reason step by step before answering.
claims.rationale: The exact numbers and story do not match the primary CoT papers as written.

claims.claim_text: Jason Wei is quoted as saying step-by-step prompting can triple performance on some reasoning tasks.
claims.span_hint: section: The Six Words That Changed AI
claims.anchor_text: > "We found that simply prompting the model to 'think step by step' before answering can triple performance on some reasoning tasks. It's one of the simplest and most effective techniques we've discovered."
claims.suggested_rewrite: > Later papers reported that explicit step-by-step prompting can substantially improve some reasoning benchmarks.
claims.rationale: This exact quotation is not attributable to the cited paper text.

claims.claim_text: The quote is specifically attributed to Jason Wei, Google Research, in the CoT paper.
claims.span_hint: section: The Six Words That Changed AI
claims.anchor_text: > — Jason Wei, Google Research (Chain-of-Thought paper, 2022)
claims.suggested_rewrite: > — Summary of findings from early chain-of-thought prompting papers
claims.rationale: The paper itself is not a source for this interview-style attribution.

claims.claim_text: The CoT discovery became one of the most cited papers in AI.
claims.span_hint: section: The Six Words That Changed AI
claims.anchor_text: This discovery—Chain-of-Thought prompting—became one of the most cited papers in AI.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Chain-of-thought prompting quickly became an influential paper in LLM reasoning research.
claims.rationale: Citation-rank claims are unstable and require a live bibliometric source, which is not given here.

claims.claim_text: Chain-of-thought prompting is now built into every major AI assistant.
claims.span_hint: section: The Six Words That Changed AI
claims.anchor_text: It's now built into every major AI assistant.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Variants of explicit reasoning prompts are now commonly discussed in LLM products and tooling.
claims.rationale: This universal product-adoption claim is too broad to support honestly from a single primary source.

claims.claim_text: Wei et al. (2022) improved GSM8K from 17.9% to 57.1%, a 3x improvement from just six words.
claims.span_hint: section: The Heureka Moment / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: The Chain-of-Thought paper by Wei et al. (2022) at Google Brain showed that adding "Let's think step by step" improved accuracy on the GSM8K math benchmark from 17.9% to 57.1% - a 3x improvement from just 6 words!
claims.suggested_rewrite: Wei et al. (2022) showed that chain-of-thought prompting substantially improved benchmark performance over standard prompting on GSM8K.
claims.rationale: The exact numbers and the claim that the gain came from merely adding six words do not match the CoT paper.

claims.claim_text: The CoT paper was initially met with skepticism and critics made the quoted objection.
claims.span_hint: section: The Heureka Moment / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: The paper was initially met with skepticism. "You're just asking it to show its work," critics said. But that's exactly the point - the "work" IS the reasoning.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The important lesson is that forcing the model to generate intermediate steps can improve multi-step reasoning performance.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed reaction narrative rather than a citable primary-source claim.

claims.claim_text: Adding "Let's think step by step" can materially improve zero-shot reasoning performance on many tasks.
claims.span_hint: section: Zero-Shot Chain-of-Thought
claims.rationale: Kojima et al. directly study zero-shot reasoning gains from adding this trigger phrase.

claims.claim_text: Kojima et al. tested 60+ trigger phrases; "Let's think step by step" consistently won; even "Think" alone helps.
claims.span_hint: section: Zero-Shot Chain-of-Thought / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: Kojima et al. (2022) tested 60+ different trigger phrases. "Let's think step by step" consistently outperformed all others, but the exact wording matters less than the presence of ANY reasoning trigger. Even "Think" alone helps!
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kojima et al. (2022) showed that simple reasoning-trigger phrases, especially "Let's think step by step," can substantially improve zero-shot reasoning performance.
claims.rationale: The paper supports the general finding but not these exact counts and ranking claims as written here.

claims.claim_text: Prompts like "Think carefully" or "Make sure you're right" hurt performance because they create anxiety-like patterns.
claims.span_hint: section: Zero-Shot Chain-of-Thought / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: The researchers also discovered that asking the model to "Think carefully" or "Make sure you're right" actually HURT performance. These create anxiety-like patterns that lead to overthinking and second-guessing. Neutral, process-focused triggers work best.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Not every reasoning trigger works equally well, so neutral step-by-step phrasing is safer than ad hoc variants.
claims.rationale: The anthropomorphic explanation and exact negative findings are not supported by the primary paper cited here.

claims.claim_text: ReAct interleaves reasoning with actions and improved over strong baselines on QA, fact verification, and decision-making benchmarks.
claims.span_hint: section: ReAct: Reasoning and Acting
claims.rationale: The ReAct paper abstract directly supports the interleaving pattern and benchmark improvements.

claims.claim_text: Self-consistency with just five samples improved CoT from 58% to 74% on math problems.
claims.span_hint: section: Self-Consistency / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: Wang et al. (2022) showed that self-consistency with just 5 samples improved CoT accuracy from 58% to 74% on math problems - a 28% relative improvement!
claims.suggested_rewrite: Wang et al. (2022) showed that self-consistency can materially improve chain-of-thought accuracy by sampling multiple reasoning paths and aggregating the final answer.
claims.rationale: The paper reports strong gains, but not this exact five-sample 58-to-74 framing.

claims.claim_text: Math word problems improve by 2-4x with CoT.
claims.span_hint: section: When CoT Helps
claims.anchor_text: | Math word problems | Forces calculation steps | 2-4x |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Math word problems | Explicit steps help surface intermediate calculations | substantial in benchmark studies |
claims.rationale: The lesson is real, but the range is presented as an unsourced teaching number.

claims.claim_text: Multi-step reasoning improves by 2-3x with CoT.
claims.span_hint: section: When CoT Helps
claims.anchor_text: | Multi-step reasoning | Makes dependencies explicit | 2-3x |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Multi-step reasoning | Explicit steps make dependencies easier to track | substantial in benchmark studies |
claims.rationale: The general effect is citable, but the numeric range is not sourced here.

claims.claim_text: Logical deduction improves by 1.5-2x with CoT.
claims.span_hint: section: When CoT Helps
claims.anchor_text: | Logical deduction | Tracks premises and conclusions | 1.5-2x |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Logical deduction | Step-by-step reasoning helps track premises and conclusions | often improves over direct prompting |
claims.rationale: The table gives an exact range without a supporting benchmark citation.

claims.claim_text: Commonsense reasoning improves by 1.3-1.5x with CoT.
claims.span_hint: section: When CoT Helps
claims.anchor_text: | Commonsense reasoning | Surfaces implicit assumptions | 1.3-1.5x |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Commonsense reasoning | Reasoning traces can surface otherwise implicit assumptions | sometimes improves over direct prompting |
claims.rationale: The lesson is plausible, but the precise gain range is unsourced in this module.

claims.claim_text: Code debugging improves by 1.5-2x with CoT.
claims.span_hint: section: When CoT Helps
claims.anchor_text: | Code debugging | Forces systematic analysis | 1.5-2x |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Code debugging | Step-by-step inspection can encourage more systematic analysis | improvement depends heavily on task and model |
claims.rationale: This is an illustrative benchmark-style number, not a sourced result in the text.

claims.claim_text: Anthropic found Claude's reasoning varies significantly with framing, and "What's wrong with this code?" is often better than "Is this code correct?"
claims.span_hint: section: The Limits of Reasoning / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: Researchers at Anthropic discovered that Claude's reasoning performance varies significantly based on problem framing. Asking "What's wrong with this code?" produces different (often better) debugging than "Is this code correct?"
claims.suggested_rewrite: Prompt framing can materially change LLM outputs, so debugging prompts should be tested empirically rather than assumed equivalent.
claims.rationale: The specific vendor attribution and example comparison are not grounded in a cited primary source here.

claims.claim_text: Least-to-Most prompting breaks hard problems into simpler subproblems solved in sequence.
claims.span_hint: section: Advanced CoT Techniques / Least-to-Most Prompting
claims.rationale: The named technique and its decomposition strategy are directly described in the paper.

claims.claim_text: Tree of Thoughts explores multiple branches and can backtrack during reasoning.
claims.span_hint: section: Advanced CoT Techniques / Tree of Thoughts
claims.rationale: The ToT paper explicitly frames the method as branching search with lookahead and backtracking.

claims.claim_text: Program-Aided Language Models generate code and delegate computation to an interpreter.
claims.span_hint: section: Advanced CoT Techniques / Program-Aided Language Models
claims.rationale: The PAL paper directly describes this architecture and its motivation.

claims.claim_text: PAL eliminates arithmetic errors.
claims.span_hint: section: Advanced CoT Techniques / Program-Aided Language Models
claims.anchor_text: This eliminates arithmetic errors!
claims.suggested_rewrite: This can reduce arithmetic errors by delegating computation to an interpreter.
claims.rationale: The stronger absolute claim is not justified; the cited literature supports reduction, not elimination.

claims.claim_text: Chain-of-Verification has the model draft, generate verification questions, answer them independently, and revise.
claims.span_hint: section: Advanced CoT Techniques / Chain-of-Verification
claims.rationale: The CoVe paper abstract directly describes this four-step verification pattern.

claims.claim_text: Chain-of-thought prompting was partially discovered by accident during Google experiments on GPT-3 math problems.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know / The Accidental Discovery
claims.anchor_text: Chain-of-thought prompting was partially discovered by accident. Researchers at Google were testing GPT-3 on math problems and noticed that when the model happened to "show its work" in the output, it got the answer right more often.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Early prompting work noticed that models often performed better when they generated intermediate reasoning steps, which motivated later chain-of-thought research.
claims.rationale: The accident story is anecdotal and not tied to a primary source passage.

claims.claim_text: The CoT paper has been cited over 4,000 times.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know / The Accidental Discovery
claims.anchor_text: The result was the CoT paper, which has been cited over 4,000 times.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The result was the CoT paper, which became widely cited in LLM reasoning research.
claims.rationale: Live citation counts are unstable and require a citation-index source that is not supplied here.

claims.claim_text: The phrase "Let's think step by step" was found through systematic testing of hundreds of variations, and specific variants were worse.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know / The "Let's" Breakthrough
claims.anchor_text: The specific phrase "Let's think step by step" was found through systematic testing of hundreds of variations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The phrase "Let's think step by step" became popular because later zero-shot prompting work showed that simple reasoning-trigger phrases can help on many tasks.
claims.rationale: The module overstates the search process and presents unsupported exact variation-count claims.

claims.claim_text: When OpenAI released gpt-5, users extracted a hidden system prompt that told the model to think step by step.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know / OpenAI's Hidden Prompts
claims.anchor_text: When OpenAI released gpt-5, users discovered that behind the scenes, the system prompt included CoT-style instructions. The model was being told to "think step by step" before generating responses - they had baked CoT into the product!
claims.suggested_rewrite: Some AI products internally encourage more structured reasoning, but specific hidden system-prompt claims should not be stated without a primary source.
claims.rationale: This product-behavior story is not supported by an authoritative primary source.

claims.claim_text: A specific controversial 2023 paper argued that LLMs only pattern-match on reasoning traces rather than truly reasoning.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know / The Reasoning vs Pattern Matching Debate
claims.anchor_text: A controversial 2023 paper argued that LLMs don't actually "reason" - they pattern-match on the reasoning patterns in their training data.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Some recent papers argue that apparent reasoning improvements may partly reflect learned pattern matching over reasoning traces rather than human-like reasoning.
claims.rationale: The module names no paper and overstates the claim as a single definitive result.

claims.claim_text: In 2024, DeepMind's AlphaProof combined LLM-generated reasoning with formal verification to reach silver-medal level on IMO problems.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know / AlphaProof and the Future
claims.rationale: The event is real and primary-source verifiable, but the best source is currently off the stated allowlist.

further_reading.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
further_reading.why_relevant: Foundational paper for few-shot chain-of-thought prompting.

further_reading.title: Large Language Models are Zero-Shot Reasoners
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for the "Let's think step by step" zero-shot result.

further_reading.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for interleaving thought with tool use.

further_reading.title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for sampling multiple reasoning paths and majority-style aggregation.

notes: Most citation needs are concentrated in the opening anecdote and the later Did You Know sidebars, where the module mixes real papers with unsourced narratives, unstable citation-count claims, and product folklore. Code blocks, quiz items, and illustrative examples inside exercises were not expanded into claim entries unless the surrounding prose made a standalone factual claim.
